coredata.dc:title: Core-shell type Tourmaline@ZnO composites equipped with carbon dots for high efficiency photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Lixin
Guo, Yun
Pan, Shengsen
Huang, Jian
Wang, Linjun
coredata.dc:description: Here we report that a novel type of Tourmaline@ZnO core-shell composite equipped with carbon dots (CDs) as multi-band light active sensitizer has been successfully designed and efficiently ultilized as the superior photocatalyst towards the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under Xenon lamp at room temperature. The bare ZnO, CDs/ZnO, Tourmaline@ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO were systemically synthesized following the facile homogeneous precipitation process and characterized in detail by XRD, FTIR, FESEM, HRTEM, UV-Vis, PL, PC and EIS investigations in succession. Bare ZnO displays as 3-dimensional hierarchical nano-flowers with large specific surface area reaching up to 62.049 m2/g by BET measurement, while the other composite products confirm the core-shell structures with CDs inlaying on ZnO layer surfaces. The photocatalytic activities of the polynary hybridations obtain effective enhancements compared to pure ZnO, the ternary system (CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO) deserves more attention for achieving the maximum (91.25%). It should been the synergistic effect on the highly efficient catalysis performances exerting by the presence of CDs modifier and the Tourmaline core, which make ZnO matrix receive more photo-generated electrons and broaden the visible light response originated from CDs, and synchronously hinder the recombination of electrons-hole pairs by the spontaneous polarization and surface electric field of Tourmaline.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnO
Tourmaline
CDs
core-shell
photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of pure ZnO and Tourmaline@ZnO
Synthesis of CDs/ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO nanocomposite
XRD pattern and FTIR spectra analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Partial XRD patterns from 10°~30°; (b) XRD patterns of the ZnO sample series; (c) FTIR spectra of the as-prepared ZnO-based samples and CDs.
(a) SEM image of bare ZnO; (b) pore size distribution of ZnO; (c) SEM image of Tourmaline@ZnO; (d-e) EDS results of Tourmaline@ZnO.

(a) The TEM image of ZnO; (b) selected area electron diffraction of ZnO; (c-d) the HRTEM images of Tourmaline@ZnO; (e-f) the HRTEM and TEM images of CDs/ZnO; (g-i) the TEM and EDS result of CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO.
(a-b) UV-Vis absorption spectrums and the transformed Kubelka-Munk function versus energy of the excitation source; (c-d) PL spectra of ZnO-based products.

(a) Photocurrent density versus time curves of samples; (b) electrochemical impedance spectra of ZnO composites; (c) photocatalytic activities; (d) kinetic curves for catalytic degradation of MO under.
The photocatalytic schematic diagram for CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The bold in the table represents the most active efficiency.

head.ce:title: Core-shell type Tourmaline@ZnO composites equipped with carbon dots for high efficiency photocatalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Shanghai University, 200444, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report that a novel type of Tourmaline@ZnO core-shell composite equipped with carbon dots (CDs) as multi-band light active sensitizer has been successfully designed and efficiently ultilized as the superior photocatalyst towards the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under Xenon lamp at room temperature. The bare ZnO, CDs/ZnO, Tourmaline@ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO were systemically synthesized following the facile homogeneous precipitation process and characterized in detail by XRD, FTIR, FESEM, HRTEM, UV-Vis, PL, PC and EIS investigations in succession. Bare ZnO displays as 3-dimensional hierarchical nano-flowers with large specific surface area reaching up to 62.049 m2/g by BET measurement, while the other composite products confirm the core-shell structures with CDs inlaying on ZnO layer surfaces. The photocatalytic activities of the polynary hybridations obtain effective enhancements compared to pure ZnO, the ternary system (CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO) deserves more attention for achieving the maximum (91.25%). It should been the synergistic effect on the highly efficient catalysis performances exerting by the presence of CDs modifier and the Tourmaline core, which make ZnO matrix receive more photo-generated electrons and broaden the visible light response originated from CDs, and synchronously hinder the recombination of electrons-hole pairs by the spontaneous polarization and surface electric field of Tourmaline.

ce:section.ce:para: After Honda and Fujishima first opened the revolutionary avenue for exploring the photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting on the TiO2 photoanode, semiconductor photoelectrodes have attracted widespread attention and got significant achievements nowadays [1–3]. With the swift and violent economy development, there are more and more organic contaminants produced by industry and life. Semiconductor materials are considerable to be used for photocatalytic degradation of these pollutants, apart from TiO2, Fe2O3, SrTiO3, ZnS, WO3 and BiFeO3, ZnO is regarded as the most excellent candidate due to its unique optical, morphological and economical performances such as low cost, simple synthesis, high electron mobility, diversity of morphology, high catalytic activity and environmental friendliness [4–6]. However, pristine ZnO, with the large energy gap of 3.37 eV, can only take use of the ultraviolet light, occupying definite parts of sunlight (less than 5%), and couple with higher recombination of photo-generated electrons and holes leading to the lower catalytic efficiency.

As ZnO-based photocatalyst considered, there are many innovative pathways to improve its photocatalytic efficiency. For example, the way of precious metal deposition doping with Ag, Au [7,8] can change the surface properties of semiconductors in order to get more photo-generated electrons, nevertheless, excessive noble metals would make themselves the fast recombination center of electrons and holes. The obvious drawback exists not only in cost-benefit but also metallic dopant can replace or introduce oxygen vacancies in ZnO lattice structure, which could make the band gap width smaller [9–11]. Additionally, other reliable strategies have also been adopted by improving preparation method, designing heterojunction [12,13] along with doping metal ions [14,15], transition metals [16,17] or rare earth elements [18,19]. In summary, increasing specific surface area, extending the response spectrum to cover more visible regions and cutting down the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole are beneficial methods of regulation and control for ZnO-based photocatalyst.

Recently, Tourmaline and carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials to increase photocatalytic efficiency of ZnO matrix as reported in several literatures [20–25]. Tourmaline is a type of earth-abundantly borosilicate compounds and natural multifunctional mineral material, trigonal space group R3m, with the chemical formula of (Na,Ca)(Mg,Fe,Mn,Li,Al)3Al6(Si6O18)(BO3)3(OH,F)4 [26,27]. Especially, micron-sized Tourmaline particles can generate a surface electric field of 106-107 V/m, which are favorable to effectively split electron-hole pairs [28–31]. While CDs, the new type of carbon nanomaterials, possess numerous advantages of simple fabrication, non-toxicity, small size, high stabilization, extensive absorption [32–35]. CDs can absorb long wavelength portion of sunlight (700 nm~900 nm) and then emit the short wave from 325 nm to 425 nm through the up-conversion mechanism, which could activate ZnO to engender more electron-hole pairs. On the other hand, owing to the photo-induced electron transfer characteristics, CDs can assist ZnO to achieve the degradation of dyes [36–38]. However, there are fewer documents regarding the multi-band absorption of CDs in the visible region. Xu et al. have ever synthetized carbon dots with multi-band light activity, but the technical process seems rather cumbersome [39].

Herein, we report a simple and facile one-step hydrothermal method to prepare the glowing red CDs with multi-band light absorption, and it is noteworthy that we first devise a novel type of Tourmaline@ZnO core-shell composite equipped by CDs, which presents superior photocatalytic activity for the degradation of methyl orange (MO) under visible light. As bare ZnO displays like the 3-dimensional hierarchical peony-flower with high specific surface area reaching up to 62.049 m2/g, which provides a great deal of loading sites with CDs and dyes. The experimental results also demonstrate that CDs upload and Tourmaline core synergistically played the key roles in the optical performances of ZnO outer shell.

All reagents were analytically pure without any further purification unless otherwise mentioned. Deionized water was used in the whole experiment. The chemical composition of Tourmaline mainly contains SiO2, CaO, B2O3, Fe2O3, FeO, Na2O, Al2O3, and MgO, which have been inspected by electron probe microscopic analyzer (EPMA) presented in our previous publication [40]. The average diameter of the Tourmaline particle is about 4.2 μm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pure ZnO and Tourmaline@ZnO
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.2 M zinc nitrate hexahydrate, 0.9 M urea and an appropriate amount of surfactants were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized water. After that, optimal amount of Tourmaline powders (3 wt%) were added in or not to prepare the Tourmaline@ZnO and pure ZnO, all solutions were shocked by ultrasonic cleaner for 10 min and heated to 100 °C for 2 h. After cooling down naturally to room temperature, the prepared samples were rinsed thrice with water and ethanol. Finally, the precipitates were dried at 70 °C and annealed at 400 °C.

CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 1 g of p-phenylenediamine and 0.6 g of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixed brown solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 2 min to remove large particles and the supernatant was kept dialyzing for 12 h. After removing solvents and freeze drying, red-brown powders of CDs were obtained about 2.2 mg and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs/ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: An amount of CDs was mixed respectively with 60 mg of pure ZnO and Tourmaline@ZnO powder (optimum Tourmaline addition amount of 3 wt%) in 10 mL of ethanol to find the optimal proportion (the CDs addition amount of 300ul and 400ul respectively) and stirred for 4 h at room temperature. The samples were then filtered and washed with water for several times, finally were dried overnight at 100 °C.

The structure identification of the as-prepared samples was investigated by XRD (D/max-111C) with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418 nm). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were detected with the Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR Spectrometer. The morphology and microstructure were examined by SEM (Hitachi S-4800) and HRTEM (TF20). Pore size distribution was recorded by BET testing (Hitachi F-4600). Photoluminescent properties were measured by the fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-4600) with the excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature. The optical absorption features were drawn by the UV–Vis investigations (Hitachi U-3900H). The photocurrent (PC) and electrochemical impedance experiment (EIS) were carried out at room temperature using Chenhua CHI 660E computer controlled electrochemical analyzer with a standard three-electrode system.

Succinctly, the photocatalytic experiments were carried out in a photochemical reactions (ZL-GXH-V) instrument under the Xenon long-arc lamp with 800 W. 40 mg of above prepared photocatalysts were dispersed in 100 mL aqueous solution of 10 mg/L methyl orange (MO). Then the reagents were transported to photocatalytic chemical reaction instrument to be stirred for 1 h in the dark to guarantee the establishment of adsorption equilibrium. During the radiation process, 4 mL aliquots were alternately extracted and centrifuged per 30 min, and then analyzed by UV-Vis spectrophotometer under 464 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD pattern and FTIR spectra analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1(a) and Fig. 1(b) are the XRD patterns of the bare ZnO, CDs/ZnO, Tourmaline@ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO. Classically, the obvious diffraction peaks positioned at 31.7°, 34.4°, 36.2°, 47.5°, 56.6°, 62.8°, 66.4°, 67.9° and 69.1°, corresponding to the standard data of ZnO (JCPDS, No. 36-1451). The indices of crystallographic plane were (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112) and (201), respectively, which confirmed that the prepared samples were ZnO phase with hexagonal wurtzite structure. Considering the XRD patterns of Tourmaline@ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO, the weak peaks existing at 10°~30° should be assigned to Tourmaline phase as issued in our previous work [41]. The diffraction peaks of CDs modifier around 10°~30° both in bare ZnO and Tourmaline@ZnO have barely been discovered owing to the tiny mass percentage (less than 5%). Typically, the average crystallite sizes are inhibited from 20.5 nm (in bare ZnO) to 17.4 nm (in Tourmaline@ZnO) calculated by Debye-Scherrer in respect that the C axis growth of ZnO was suppressed by the spontaneous polarization of Tourmaline. While the grain sizes between pure ZnO and CDs/ZnO have not altered, similarly between Tourmaline@ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO, which means CDs introduction did not change structure of ZnO.

Fig. 1(c) presents the FTIR spectra of the obtained materials. ZnO exhibits main peaks at 3500 cm-1, 1131 cm-1 and 503 cm-1 respectively. ZnO stretching vibration lies at 503 cm-1 [42]. The 3500 cm-1 absorption is the characteristic peak of H2O or OH, while 1131 cm-1 absorption is the peak of some impurities of ZnCO3. After the recombination with ZnO and Tourmaline@ZnO, these extra peaks, most notably near 2900 cm-1 and 1600 cm-1–1200 cm-1 are mostly interrelated with CDs. The above results of FTIR further indicate that the CDs/ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO composites have been successfully synthesized.

As seen in Fig. 2(a), the surface morphology of ZnO presents as 3-dimensional and hierarchical flower with micro-size scale. Interestingly, their flaky petals turned out to be not flattened but porous evidently in the top right illustration. It can be assumed that the nanoparticles were self-assembled into the porous petals, and further formed the flowery micro-morphologies. The average pore size was investigated to be 11.32 nm by BET measurement in Fig. 2(b). The large specific surface area of ZnO from BET reaches up to 62.049 m2/g, which was much benefited photocatalytic efficiency for the large specific surface area [43]. In Fig. 3(c), ZnO shaped as nano-flakes overlaying on the surface of Tourmaline to form the wrapping layer. Tourmaline is a kind of particular functional material with spontaneous polarization and surface electrical field. With the tourmaline introduction, ZnO will attach to the tourmaline surface because of the electrostatic attraction mechanism and chemical precipitation effect. The preferential growth along C axis of ZnO have been inhibited due to the surface electric field of tourmaline core, thus affect the morphology of ZnO in the shell layer. EDS testing results in Fig. 2(d) and (e) distinctly verified the chemical content difference between the outer shell and the inner core.

For further research, HRTEM technology was implemented to explore the fine morphologies. Average particle size of ZnO in Fig. 3(a) is estimated to be about 15 nm. The microscopic feature with hexagonal wurtzite structure is demonstrated by selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) shown in Fig. 3(b), in accord with the results of the XRD calculation. The diffraction pattern is circular rather than lattice-like, indicating the ZnO polycrystalline nature. As it is worth mentioning that the interplanar spacing of 0.2824 nm and 0.2632 nm clearly observed in Fig. 3(c-d) correspond to (100) and (002) crystallographic planes of ZnO, while 0.3248 nm is equivalent to the (002) spacing plane of CDs and the dots diameter quoted out are close to be 5 nm, a convincing evidence for the modification of CDs with ZnO in Fig. 3(e-f), as ZnO and CDs formed a stable heterojunction [44,45]. The core-shell structure of Tourmaline@ZnO has been well designed as seen in Fig. 3(g-h). The boundary of core-shell is also easy to discern out depending on the contrast difference, the Tourmaline core is wrapped by ZnO thin shell, the multi component CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO are further exemplified by EDS analysis as well as the existence of C element in Fig. 3(i).

Fig. 4(a) depicts the UV-Vis absorption spectrums of the ZnO-based composite samples, with strong absorption in the ultraviolet region but weak and wide absorption in visible light [46]. For the Tourmaline@ZnO, Tourmaline had almost no effect on ZnO for UV-Vis properties except that it caused absorption in the far infrared region. Compared CDs/ZnO and CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO products with bare ZnO, the weak and wide absorptical bulges between 400 nm and 600 nm indicate the incorporation of the CDs into ZnO can widen the optical absorption towards the visible light region. The bandgaps of all samples direct to the same value about 3.0 eV without obvious shift in Fig. 4(b), as the small amount of CDs adhered to the surface of ZnO nanoparticles does not affect the energy structure of ZnO.

As known that, the peak intensity of PL spectroscopy reflects the recombination degree of the carriers [47]. Fig. 4(c-d) represents PL spectra under the excitation wavelength of 325 nm at room temperature occasion. All synthesized products display the near band edge emission (NBE) at 395 nm, which demonstrated the excitonic recombination of charge carriers [48]. The weaker the NBE peak is, the less the recombination degree does. Tourmaline and CDs have synergistic effects on the separation of electron-holes obviously shown in Fig. 4(d), which make the NBE peaks weaken. Besides, the intensities of lattice defect peaks around 413 nm, probably Zinc interstitial (Zni) or oxygen vacancy (VO) defects, suggest the crystallinity of ZnO improved by Tourmaline rather than CDs, which is favorable for photocatalytic activity while exciton recombination does conversely [49].

The charge transfer properties of CDs and Tourmaline composites with ZnO were also approved by PC and EIS experiments. Generally, the larger the photo-current in PC and the smaller arc radius in an EIS Nyquist plot perform, the better the separation effect of the photogenerated electron-hole pair achieve [50,51], we have recorded the PC and EIS curves of above samples in an on-and-off cycle mode under the Xenon lamp illumination. CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO has evidently the larger photo-current compared to the others in Fig. 5(a), resulting in higher photocatalytic activities. Meanwhile, the results of EIS with the same equivalent circuit model presented in Fig. 5(b) are well in accordance with PC results, where the Rs, Rct and CPE represent the solution resistance, charge transfer resistance and constant phase element respectively. Therefore, CDs and Tourmaline have been approved to be the beneficial interrupter against the recombination of electrons and holes, so as to play synergistic effect on enhancing the photocatalytic activity concerning ZnO matrix.

As shown in Fig. 5(c), it turns out that almost no degradation occurs under visible light in the case of absent photocatalyst accordingly. All the ZnO-based products manifest their considerable photolytic performances, while CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO attains the optimum with the efficiency approaching 91.25% within two hours. Fig. 5(d) reveals the as-prepared samples follow the pseudo-first-order kinetics, ln(C 0/C
t ) = kt, where C
t is the concentration at specific time, C 0 the initial concentration of the MO solution, and the slope k the apparent reaction rate constant. The kinetics data are listed in Table 1 , as R 2 is the goodness of fitting. Compared with bare ZnO, the degradation rate of CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO has increased by 54%, strongly verify that CDs and Tourmaline have played pivotal roles in the photocatalytic process leading the ternary CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO to reach the fastest reaction rate.

As shown in Fig. 6 , there are four key steps in the heterogeneous photocatalytic mechanism of semiconductor surface. (1) When CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO was irradiated, ZnO produced electron hole pairs, but some of them would recombine; (2) because of the surface electric field of tourmaline, the recombination of electrons and holes was reduced for ZnO; (3) furthermore, the CDs on the surface of ZnO accelerated the transfer of electrons. These electrons and holes reacted with the solution to form some hydroxyl radicals (OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 -); (4) target pollutants were decomposed into CO2 and H2O by these active groups. The reactive mechanism for photocatalytic activity can be announced as follows [52,53]:
ZnO + hv → ZnO +
h +
+
e −
a
H 2
O +
H +
→ ˙ OH +
H +
b
˙ OH +
H +
→ ˙ OH
c
O 2
+
e −
→ ˙
O 2
−
d
H +
+ ˙
O 2
−
→
HO 2
˙
e
2
HO 2
˙ →
H 2
O 2
+
O 2
f
H 2
O 2
+ hv → ˙ OH
g

In summary, bare ZnO shapes as the 3-dimensional hierarchical nanoflower with large specific surface area up to 62.049 m2/g. Multi-band light active CDs has been successfully designed via a simple hydrothermal method and ultilized as the superior modifier for ZnO catalysts, the tourmaline core and CDs modifier are beneficial for ZnO-based material to enhance the photocatalytic ability. Most of all, the ternary CDs/Tourmaline@ZnO we initiatorily prepared exhibits the most excellent photocatalytic efficency towards the degradation of MO dye, whereas UV-Vis absorption indicates it has provided a better response to visible light compared with the others. Furthermore, Tourmaline and CDs have synchronously prevented the recombination of electrons-hole pairs after the careful evaluations of PL, PC, and EIS analysis, which offer a new approach to develop highly effective composite photocatalysts based on ZnO marix.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors sincerely acknowledge the funding support from Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai (No. 16110500500).
